title: The difference between top-down and bottom-up processing in recovery
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
Your brain thinks it's in charge 
 I spent years trying to think my way out of trauma. I read books. I journaled. I made lists of cognitive distortions an...
htmlContent:
Your brain thinks it's in charge

I spent years trying to think my way out of trauma. I read books. I journaled. I made lists of cognitive distortions and challenged every negative thought until my head throbbed. And you know what happened? Nothing. Well, not nothing. I got better at explaining why I felt terrible. But I still felt terrible.

Here's the thing. Your brain has a top floor and a bottom floor. The top floor is your prefrontal cortex. That's the part that plans, reasons, and tells itself nice things in the mirror. The bottom floor is your brainstem and limbic system. That's the part that detects threats, regulates your heartbeat, and decides whether you're safe or dying before your conscious mind even gets a vote.

Most recovery approaches start at the top. They hand you a worksheet and ask you to reframe your thoughts. They tell you to breathe slowly and visualize a peaceful beach. And for some people, that works. For people whose nervous systems are already regulated, top-down processing can be a useful tool. But if you're reading this, I'm guessing you're not one of those people.

When your nervous system is stuck in survival mode, your top floor is a hostage. You can tell yourself "I am safe" a thousand times. Your body doesn't care. Your body is running ancient software that prioritizes survival over happiness. And it won't listen to your prefrontal cortex's PowerPoint presentation about how the threat is over.

This is where bottom-up processing comes in. Bottom-up means starting with the body. The sensations. The breath in your lungs. The tension in your jaw. The flutter in your chest. You don't try to change what you're thinking. You notice what you're feeling. Physically. Right here, right now.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. She's a clinician who studied directly with Stephen Porges, the man behind polyvagal theory. Dana breaks down how to map your nervous system's patterns without judgment. It's not about fixing. It's about noticing. Wild, right?

The problem with talking about your trauma

I'm not anti-talk therapy. I've done years of it. I've sat in chairs and told strangers about my childhood until I was hoarse. And some of it helped. But here's what I noticed. I could talk about the worst moments of my life while my body remained completely frozen. My voice stayed flat. My hands stayed still. I was reporting on my trauma like a news anchor reading a script about a disaster in another country.

Bessel van der Kolk, the psychiatrist who wrote "The Body Keeps the Score," has spent decades showing us that trauma isn't stored in the narrative parts of the brain. It's stored in the body. In the amygdala. In the vagus nerve. In the muscles that stay clenched years after the danger passed. You can tell the story a hundred times and your nervous system never changes because you never actually visited the body.

Bottom-up processing asks you to do something harder than talking. It asks you to feel. To track sensations without trying to change them. To notice the heat in your chest without calling it anxiety. To feel the trembling in your legs without bracing against it. This is what Peter Levine calls pendulation. Moving between activation and settling. Letting the body complete its unfinished survival responses.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link). Levine wrote it. It's his explanation of how the body holds and releases trauma. It's not an easy read. But it's an honest one. And honesty is what we need, not more platitudes.

Why your thinking brain keeps failing you

Let me get direct with you. Your thinking brain is not equipped to handle trauma. It evolved to solve problems. To make plans. To figure out where the next meal is coming from. It did not evolve to process the feeling of being unsafe in a body that is supposed to be safe. When you try to think your way out of a dysregulated nervous system, you're asking a fish to climb a tree. It's not that the fish is broken. It's that you're using the wrong tool.

Here's what top-down processing looks like in practice. You feel a wave of panic. Your heart races. Your palms sweat. Your mind starts scanning for threats. And then your prefrontal cortex kicks in and says "There's no real danger. You're fine. This is just anxiety. Calm down." And maybe you do calm down for a minute. But the panic comes back. Because you never addressed the signal coming from your body. You just overrode it temporarily.

Bottom-up processing looks different. You feel the panic. You notice your heart racing. You notice your palms sweating. You notice the urge to escape. And instead of telling yourself to calm down, you put your hand on your chest. You feel the rhythm of your heartbeat. You notice the temperature of your skin. You let your body know, through sensation, that you are here and you are paying attention. You are not running. You are not fighting. You are staying.

This is the core of what Janina Fisher teaches. She's a trauma specialist who integrates parts work with somatic approaches. She says that the traumatized parts of us need to be witnessed, not argued with. Your panic isn't an error to be corrected. It's a part of you that needs to know it's not alone. Sit with that.

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link) by Oprah Winfrey and Bruce Perry. Perry is a neuroscientist who studies how early experiences shape the brain. The book asks a different question. Not "what's wrong with you?" but "what happened to you?" That shift alone can change how you approach your own healing.

How to start with bottom-up processing

You don't need a special room or an expensive therapist to start. You just need your body and a few minutes of willingness. Here's a practice I use when I feel my nervous system ramping up. I find a place where I can sit or lie down without being interrupted. I close my eyes. I take one breath. And then I ask myself one question: "What do I notice right now?"

Not what do I think. Not what story is my mind telling me. What do I notice. Physically. I scan from the top of my head to the bottom of my feet. I notice the weight of my body against the floor. I notice the air touching my skin. I notice any places that feel tight, heavy, hot, cold, tingly, or numb. I don't try to change anything. I just notice. Stay with me here.

This is called orienting. It's a bottom-up technique that tells your nervous system "I am here, in this room, in this moment." When you orient, you activate the ventral vagal pathway. That's the social engagement system that Stephen Porges talks about. It's the part of your nervous system that feels safe enough to connect. You can't force it. But you can invite it by paying attention to your body in a gentle, curious way.

Notice what just happened in your body as you read that. Did you take a deeper breath? Did your shoulders drop even a little? Did you feel something shift? That's bottom-up processing happening in real time. You didn't think your way into that shift. You felt your way there. Your body knew what to do. It just needed you to stop overriding it with your thinking brain.

The messy middle of integration

I don't want to give you the impression that bottom-up processing is a magic bullet. It's not. It's hard. It's uncomfortable. When you start paying attention to your body, you will feel things you've been avoiding for years. That tightness in your chest? It might be grief. That heaviness in your legs? It might be rage. That numbness in your hands? It might be the place where you learned to disconnect from your own aliveness.

Top-down and bottom-up are not enemies. They're partners. The goal isn't to abandon your thinking brain. The goal is to let your body lead and let your thinking brain support. You use your prefrontal cortex to notice what's happening in your body. You use it to name the sensations. You use it to make meaning. But you don't use it to override or control.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link) by Deb Dana. It's full of exercises that bridge the gap between understanding your nervous system and actually working with it. You'll learn to track your states, map your triggers, and find your glimmers. Those are the small moments of safety that your nervous system can learn to .

Here's what I want you to take away from this. Your thinking brain is not the enemy. But it's not the hero either. It's a helpful assistant that got promoted to CEO and now the whole company is in chaos. You need to put your body back in charge. Not because your body is perfect. But because your body is where the trauma lives. And that's also where the healing lives. You can't heal what you won't visit.

So try this today. Stop reading. Put your hand on your chest. Feel your heartbeat. Feel the rise and fall of your breath. Don't change it. Don't judge it. Just be with it for ten seconds. That's bottom-up processing. That's the beginning. And it's enough.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Anchored (paid link) - A practical guide to working with your nervous system
  • What Happened to You? (paid link) - A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many
  • In an Unspoken Voice (paid link) - Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing
  • The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link) - A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical
